IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—obtaining NER for target languages with no human annotation—by proposing two complementary weakly supervised approaches (annotation projection with a heuristic selection scheme and representation projection via embedding mappings). Empirically it outperforms prior cross-lingual NER methods on CoNLL data and demonstrates a useful capability: projecting target embeddings into English lets a single English NER model decode multiple languages without retraining. These ideas and the proposed co-decoding schemes are likely to be adopted and extended, opening clear follow-on work on better projection, selection, and combination strategies. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of cross-lingual NER without any human annotation in the target language, proposing two complementary approaches (annotation projection with heuristic sentence selection and representation projection via embedding mappings) and combined co-decoding. Empirically it outperforms prior cross-lingual methods on CoNLL data and shows the practical benefit that a single English NER model can decode multiple target languages after embedding projection. These contributions are likely to influence how researchers build weakly supervised multilingual NER systems and to spur follow-up work on projection-based transfer and data-selection heuristics, though reliance on English as the common space and omission of gazetteer/character features temper the breadth of immediate applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—obtaining NER for target languages with no human annotation—by proposing two complementary weakly supervised approaches (annotation projection with a heuristic selection scheme and representation projection via embedding mappings). Empirically it outperforms prior cross-lingual NER methods on CoNLL data and demonstrates a useful capability: projecting target embeddings into English lets a single English NER model decode multiple languages without retraining. These ideas and the proposed co-decoding schemes are likely to be adopted and extended, opening clear follow-on work on better projection, selection, and combination strategies.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of cross-lingual NER without any human annotation in the target language, proposing two complementary approaches (annotation projection with heuristic sentence selection and representation projection via embedding mappings) and combined co-decoding. Empirically it outperforms prior cross-lingual methods on CoNLL data and shows the practical benefit that a single English NER model can decode multiple target languages after embedding projection. These contributions are likely to influence how researchers build weakly supervised multilingual NER systems and to spur follow-up work on projection-based transfer and data-selection heuristics, though reliance on English as the common space and omission of gazetteer/character features temper the breadth of immediate applicability.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: cross-lingual NER is a core NLP problem and the paper proposes two weakly supervised approaches (annotation projection with sentence selection and representation projection via embedding mappings) evaluated on standard CoNLL datasets and additional in-house languages. The methods, architectures (NN1/NN2), co-decoding schemes, and comparisons to established baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) use the evaluation standards (precision/recall/F1) expected for these conferences. The problem formulation, datasets, and positioning indicate the right audience and community (cross-lingual/multilingual NLP) for a top-tier venue. | B(5): This is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: weakly supervised cross-lingual NER is a mainstream NLP problem with broad interest. The paper targets the right audience by proposing transferable methods (annotation projection, representation projection, co-decoding) and introducing neural architectures, and it evaluates them with expected standards (CoNLL datasets, in-house languages, comparisons to Täckström et al. and Tsai et al., and F1/Precision/Recall metrics). The problem formulation, use of standard benchmarks and baselines, and explicit discussion of limitations (no target annotations, no gazetteers) show it is well positioned for the venue’s research community.

The methods, architectures (NN1/NN2), co-decoding schemes, and comparisons to established baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) use the evaluation standards (precision/recall/F1) expected for these conferences. The problem formulation, datasets, and positioning indicate the right audience and community (cross-lingual/multilingual NLP) for a top-tier venue.

The paper targets the right audience by proposing transferable methods (annotation projection, representation projection, co-decoding) and introducing neural architectures, and it evaluates them with expected standards (CoNLL datasets, in-house languages, comparisons to Täckström et al. and Tsai et al., and F1/Precision/Recall metrics). The problem formulation, use of standard benchmarks and baselines, and explicit discussion of limitations (no target annotations, no gazetteers) show it is well positioned for the venue’s research community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments cover multiple target languages (CoNLL Spanish/Dutch/German plus in-house Japanese/Korean/Portuguese), compare against prior cross-lingual baselines, evaluate both proposed methods (annotation projection with selection and representation projection with NN1/NN2) and test co-decoding schemes, which supports the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported in the summary (e.g., detailed ablation of NN1 vs NN2, selection thresholds, or mapping quality), and code availability is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. The paper does acknowledge key limitations (no gazetteers/char features, noisy projection labels, reliance on mapping to English) and related work coverage is described as adequate, so overall the evaluation is solid with some minor gaps. | B(4): The paper evaluates methods across multiple target languages (CoNLL Spanish/Dutch/German plus in-house Japanese/Korean/Portuguese), compares to prior cross-lingual baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) and reports CoNLL test improvements, which provides solid experimental support for the main claims. It analyzes the annotation-projection sentence selection (entity-frequency-based quality scores) and presents two NN architectures and co-decoding schemes, but the summary does not clearly report thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating each component’s contribution. Related work coverage is described as adequate and several limitations are acknowledged (no gazetteers/char features, noisy projection labels, reliance on mapping into English); hyperparameters are reported though code availability is not mentioned, so reproducibility is reasonable but not fully ensured.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments cover multiple target languages (CoNLL Spanish/Dutch/German plus in-house Japanese/Korean/Portuguese), compare against prior cross-lingual baselines, evaluate both proposed methods (annotation projection with selection and representation projection with NN1/NN2) and test co-decoding schemes, which supports the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported in the summary (e.g., detailed ablation of NN1 vs NN2, selection thresholds, or mapping quality), and code availability is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. The paper does acknowledge key limitations (no gazetteers/char features, noisy projection labels, reliance on mapping to English) and related work coverage is described as adequate, so overall the evaluation is solid with some minor gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates methods across multiple target languages (CoNLL Spanish/Dutch/German plus in-house Japanese/Korean/Portuguese), compares to prior cross-lingual baselines (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.) and reports CoNLL test improvements, which provides solid experimental support for the main claims. It analyzes the annotation-projection sentence selection (entity-frequency-based quality scores) and presents two NN architectures and co-decoding schemes, but the summary does not clearly report thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating each component’s contribution. Related work coverage is described as adequate and several limitations are acknowledged (no gazetteers/char features, noisy projection labels, reliance on mapping into English); hyperparameters are reported though code availability is not mentioned, so reproducibility is reasonable but not fully ensured.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper combines established ideas (annotation projection and bilingual embedding mapping) but contributes moderate novelty through a practical heuristic for selecting high-quality projection-labeled sentences and by proposing two NN architectures plus co-decoding schemes to merge annotation- and representation-projection outputs. Training linear mappings to project multiple target-language embeddings into English to enable decoding with a single English model without retraining is a useful application-specific innovation, but it is a non-radical extension of prior cross-lingual embedding and projection methods. Overall the work is an incremental yet well-differentiated advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. | B(3): The paper combines known paradigms—annotation projection and cross-lingual embedding projection—rather than introducing a fundamentally new paradigm. Its novelty lies in practical, non-trivial engineering: a heuristic entity-frequency-derived sentence selection for noisy projection labels, linear mapping of target embeddings into English to permit a single English NER model to decode multiple languages, and two co-decoding schemes to fuse outputs. These contributions are useful and clearly differentiated from prior work (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.), but represent an incremental advance rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines established ideas (annotation projection and bilingual embedding mapping) but contributes moderate novelty through a practical heuristic for selecting high-quality projection-labeled sentences and by proposing two NN architectures plus co-decoding schemes to merge annotation- and representation-projection outputs. Training linear mappings to project multiple target-language embeddings into English to enable decoding with a single English model without retraining is a useful application-specific innovation, but it is a non-radical extension of prior cross-lingual embedding and projection methods. Overall the work is an incremental yet well-differentiated advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines known paradigms—annotation projection and cross-lingual embedding projection—rather than introducing a fundamentally new paradigm. Its novelty lies in practical, non-trivial engineering: a heuristic entity-frequency-derived sentence selection for noisy projection labels, linear mapping of target embeddings into English to permit a single English NER model to decode multiple languages, and two co-decoding schemes to fuse outputs. These contributions are useful and clearly differentiated from prior work (Täckström et al., Tsai et al.), but represent an incremental advance rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments: representation projection via linear mapping plus a single English NER (NN1/NN2) is shown to transfer to multiple target languages, and co-decoding of annotation-projection and representation-projection outputs outperforms the two cited baselines on CoNLL test sets. The methodology (annotation projection with entity-frequency-based sentence selection; monolingual embeddings with learned linear projection) is technically sound and appropriate for the claims. Minor concerns are the absence of reported statistical significance testing and a relatively small set of baseline comparisons, which limit confidence in the generality of the improvements but do not invalidate the experimental support presented. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental evidence: the paper evaluates annotation-projection with a sentence-quality selection heuristic and a representation-projection approach (linear embedding mappings + NN1/NN2) and reports that combined co-decoding systems outperform two prior cross-lingual NER baselines on CoNLL test data. There are no complex mathematical derivations to check, and the use of linear mappings for embedding projection is standard and consistent with the methodology described. Methodologically the experiments use appropriate datasets (CoNLL and in-house data) and reasonable baselines, but the summary does not report statistical significance testing and only two prior methods are compared, which are minor gaps that do not overturn the results. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, modulo these limited experimental-analysis issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments: representation projection via linear mapping plus a single English NER (NN1/NN2) is shown to transfer to multiple target languages, and co-decoding of annotation-projection and representation-projection outputs outperforms the two cited baselines on CoNLL test sets. The methodology (annotation projection with entity-frequency-based sentence selection; monolingual embeddings with learned linear projection) is technically sound and appropriate for the claims. Minor concerns are the absence of reported statistical significance testing and a relatively small set of baseline comparisons, which limit confidence in the generality of the improvements but do not invalidate the experimental support presented.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental evidence: the paper evaluates annotation-projection with a sentence-quality selection heuristic and a representation-projection approach (linear embedding mappings + NN1/NN2) and reports that combined co-decoding systems outperform two prior cross-lingual NER baselines on CoNLL test data. There are no complex mathematical derivations to check, and the use of linear mappings for embedding projection is standard and consistent with the methodology described. Methodologically the experiments use appropriate datasets (CoNLL and in-house data) and reasonable baselines, but the summary does not report statistical significance testing and only two prior methods are compared, which are minor gaps that do not overturn the results. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, modulo these limited experimental-analysis issues.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear intro, methods, experiments and conclusion—the main contributions are stated early and the methodology is described step-by-step (annotation projection, representation projection, co-decoding). Notation and experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported and thus appear consistent and well defined; related work coverage is adequate. Writing quality is described as good with no major grammatical issues, so only minor presentation issues are implied rather than major clarity problems. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from methods (annotation and representation projection) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (proposed two approaches, two NN architectures, and co-decoding schemes). Notation and methodological description seem consistent and sufficiently detailed for reproduction (hyperparameters reported, datasets and baselines listed). One minor uncertainty is that the summary does not explicitly report the quality of figures/tables or examples, but overall the writing quality is described as good, supporting a generally clear presentation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear intro, methods, experiments and conclusion—the main contributions are stated early and the methodology is described step-by-step (annotation projection, representation projection, co-decoding). MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against established prior methods (Tä c kström et al. 2012 and Tsai et al. 2016) and evaluates on standard CoNLL test sets using F1/Precision/Recall, which is appropriate. Comparisons appear largely fair because the same datasets and metrics are reported (and they also report results on in-house annotated languages), but the summary does not clarify whether all baselines used identical resources/features (e.g., gazetteers or character-level features), so there is a minor potential fairness concern. The authors discuss differences (annotation vs. representation projection, heuristic selection) and related work coverage is described as adequate, though inclusion of more recent competing methods would strengthen completeness. | B(4): The paper compares against two established cross-lingual NER baselines (Täckström et al. 2012; Tsai et al. 2016) using standard CoNLL test sets and evaluation metrics (F1/precision/recall), suggesting fair experimental conditions. It also reports hyperparameters and claims combined systems outperform those prior approaches. However, only two (relatively older) baselines are included, and the summary does not indicate inclusion of more recent neural or stronger competing methods nor explicitly state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better. Related work coverage is noted as adequate, so this is a solid but not fully comprehensive comparison.

The authors discuss differences (annotation vs. representation projection, heuristic selection) and related work coverage is described as adequate, though inclusion of more recent competing methods would strengthen completeness.

It also reports hyperparameters and claims combined systems outperform those prior approaches. However, only two (relatively older) baselines are included, and the summary does not indicate inclusion of more recent neural or stronger competing methods nor explicitly state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better. Related work coverage is noted as adequate, so this is a solid but not fully comprehensive comparison.

main_claims: Propose two weakly supervised cross-lingual NER approaches that require no human annotation in the target language: (1) annotation projection with a heuristic data selection scheme, and (2) representation projection that maps target-language embeddings into English for direct model transfer.

Introduce two neural network architectures (NN1 and NN2) for representation-projection-based NER and show that projection of embeddings with a single English NER model enables decoding in multiple target languages without retraining.

Design two co-decoding schemes (confidence/exclude-O and rank-based) to combine outputs of annotation projection and representation projection, and show that combined systems outperform two prior cross-lingual NER approaches on CoNLL test data.
Demonstrate that heuristic selection based on entity-frequency-derived quality scores can effectively select higher-quality projection-labeled sentences from noisy annotation-projection data.
_summary.methodology: Create weakly labeled target-language NER data by annotation projection on comparable corpora and select high-quality sentences using a per-sentence quality score averaged from entity tagging frequencies; separately, train monolingual embeddings and learn linear mappings to project target-language embeddings into English, then apply an English neural NER (NN1/NN2) to the projected inputs and combine both approaches via co-decoding.
datasets: In-house human-annotated NER data (Japanese, Korean, German, Portuguese)
Projection-labeled corpora for Spanish (2.26M tokens), Dutch (3.02M tokens), German (1.17M tokens)
limitations: Annotation projection produces noisy labels and thus requires a heuristic data selection scheme to find good-quality weakly labeled sentences.
The models do not use gazetteers or character-level representations, as these features might not be readily available or transferable across languages.
The paper does not create inter-lingual representations directly but instead trains mappings to English (i.e., relies on projecting into a single common language).
